evidence_snippets: ['iv) that an article is composed of words each pronounced singly and separately, thus: "Your passion, your voice, your look, have struck terror into your foes." Hence matters of Christian faith are said to contain distinct articles, in so far as they are divided into parts, and fit together']

['Accordingly, since there is a communication between man and God , inasmuch as He communicates His happiness to us, some kind of friendship must needs be based on this same communication, of which it is written ( 1 Corinthians 1:9 ): " God is faithful: by Whom you are called unto the fellowship of His Son." The love which is based on this communication, is charity : wherefore it is evident that charity is the friendship of man for God .']
['Wherefore, it follows that charity is a virtue , for, since charity attains God , it unites us to God , as evidenced by the authority of Augustine quoted above.']

['Accordingly if we consider, in faith , the formal aspect of the object, it is nothing else than the First Truth']
['There is a false prudence , which takes its name from its likeness to true prudence']
['It is in this sense that justice is called a general virtue']
['Therefore the proper act of justice is nothing else than to render to each one his own.']

['But since we are said to be just through doing something aright, and because the proximate principle of action is the appetitive power, justice must needs be in some appetitive power as its subject']
['A human virtue is one "which renders a human act and man himself good " [Ethic']
['Therefore every law is ordained to the common good .']
['Consequently it follows that law is something pertaining to reason .']
['Wherefore promulgation is necessary for the law to obtain its force']

['Accordingly, just as in addition to legal justice there is a need for particular virtues to direct man in relation to himself, such as temperance and fortitude , so too besides legal justice there is need for particular justice to direct man in his relations to other individuals .']

['Accordingly, since it belongs to prudence rightly to counsel, judge, and command concerning the means of obtaining a due end, it is evident that prudence regards not only the private good of the individual , but also the common good of the multitude.']
['Hence that which is the chief act of reason in regard to action must needs be the chief act of prudence']

['Hence it is evident that prudence resides only in the practical reason.']
['x): "A prudent man is one who sees as it were from afar, for his sight is keen, and he foresees the event of uncertainties." Now sight belongs not to the appetitive but to the cognitive faculty']
['Hence prudence has the nature of virtue not only as the other intellectual virtues have it, but also as the moral virtues have it, among which virtues it is enumerated.']

['Accordingly three things are found to be contrary to virtue']

['Now it is evident, in accordance with what has been said ( Article 7 ), that it is charity which directs the acts of all other virtues to the last end, and which, consequently, also gives the form to all other acts of virtue : and it is precisely in this sense that charity is called the form of the virtues , for these are called virtues in relation to "informed" acts.']
['Therefore human virtues are habits .']
['Consequently it is essential to human virtue to be an operative habit .']
